coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent-tagged water with carbon dots derived from phenylenediamine as an equipment-free nanotracer for enhanced oil recovery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ranjbar, Saba
Sarlak, Nahid
Rashidi, Alimorad
coredata.dc:description: Chemical enhanced oil recovery (EOR) through waterflooding is the most commonly used method to improve crude oil displacement and extraction however; the impact of environmental side effects may remain ambiguous. Regarding, flooding tagged water with tracers provides a better understanding of the fate of injected water and the reservoir conditions more than oil recovery. This study's main focus is the proposed carbon dots (CDs) to develop fluorescent-tagged with dual functions as a sensing and an enhancing agent for EOR operations. Different physicochemical and optical properties were obtained for CDs by tuning the surface chemistry of phenylenediamine (PD) isomers and tartaric acid (TA) via the solvothermal method which leads to green, and yellow fluorescent emissions. Size distribution and colloidal and thermal stability of the prepared nanofluids carrying CDs were controlled by atomic force microscope (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), zeta potential, and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Long-time emission stability in high temperature and salinity such as conditions found in the oil reservoirs was precisely detected by fluorescence spectroscopy and a portable UV cabinet as the on-site detection method to confirm the sensing ability of CDs. While, rheological parameters of nanofluids such as viscosity, wettability alteration, and fluid/crude oil interfacial tension were evaluated to support the potential of CDs as an enhancing agent to sweep crude oil on the carbonate rock reservoirs. The oil displacement mechanism was monitored on the micromodel pattern by recording 27.8 % and 20.5 % displacement factors for the prepared nanofluids carrying 200 ppm CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-color emission
Nanotracer
Tagged-water
Wettability alteration
Enhanced oil recovery

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: EOR nanofluid and core samples preparation
Tractability of CDs in lab scale

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of (a) g-CDs and (b) g-CDs, (c) TEM images of g-CDs, (d) Raman spectra (e) FT-IR spectra, and (f) TGA curves for as-synthesized CDs.
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra for 200 ppm of (a) g-CDs and (b) y-CDs, and fluorescence images at different concentrations of (c) g-CDs and (d) y-CDs under 365 UV lamp.

(a, b) Fluorescence spectra of g-CDs and y-CDs prepared in DW and brine, the inset shows the corresponding images taken under a 365 nm UV lamp, and (c, d) fluorescence spectra of prepared g-CDs and y-CDs in brine at room and reservoir temperature.
(a, b) Plot of fluorescence vs time and (c, d) fluorescence images for 20 and 200 ppm concentrations of g-CDs and y-CDs (photos were taken under 365 UV lamp).
(a, b) Photos taken under visible light, and 365 UV lamp, and fluorescence spectra of (c) g-CDs and (d) y-CDs at different salinity and temperature conditions.

(a) Photos taken under 365 UV lamp for (a) oil-wet core, nanofluids injected on the core samples with 200 ppm concentration of (b) g-CDs and (c) y-CDs, and (d) fluorescence spectra of collected samples after interaction with the oil-wet cores.

Contact angle of crude oil dropped on the (a) oil-wet core surface and core surface exposed in (b) brine, and nanofluids incorporated (c) 20 ppm g-CDs, (d) 200 ppm g-CDs, (e) 20 ppm y-CDs and (f) 200 ppm y-CDs, measurements of IFT by pendant drops of crude oil in, (g) brine solution, (h) 20 ppm g-CDs and (i) 20 ppm y-CDs in brine.
Images of the micromodel (a) under initial conditions, after injection of (b) brine, (c) 200 ppm g-CDs and (d) 200 ppm y-CDs in brine.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent-tagged water with carbon dots derived from phenylenediamine as an equipment-free nanotracer for enhanced oil recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), Tehran 14857-33111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), Tehran 14857-33111, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Lorestan University, Khorramabad, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Lorestan University, Khorramabad, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), Tehran 14857-33111, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical enhanced oil recovery (EOR) through waterflooding is the most commonly used method to improve crude oil displacement and extraction however; the impact of environmental side effects may remain ambiguous. Regarding, flooding tagged water with tracers provides a better understanding of the fate of injected water and the reservoir conditions more than oil recovery. This study's main focus is the proposed carbon dots (CDs) to develop fluorescent-tagged with dual functions as a sensing and an enhancing agent for EOR operations. Different physicochemical and optical properties were obtained for CDs by tuning the surface chemistry of phenylenediamine (PD) isomers and tartaric acid (TA) via the solvothermal method which leads to green, and yellow fluorescent emissions. Size distribution and colloidal and thermal stability of the prepared nanofluids carrying CDs were controlled by atomic force microscope (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), zeta potential, and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Long-time emission stability in high temperature and salinity such as conditions found in the oil reservoirs was precisely detected by fluorescence spectroscopy and a portable UV cabinet as the on-site detection method to confirm the sensing ability of CDs. While, rheological parameters of nanofluids such as viscosity, wettability alteration, and fluid/crude oil interfacial tension were evaluated to support the potential of CDs as an enhancing agent to sweep crude oil on the carbonate rock reservoirs. The oil displacement mechanism was monitored on the micromodel pattern by recording 27.8 % and 20.5 % displacement factors for the prepared nanofluids carrying 200 ppm CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 1950s, tracer technology revolutionized the upstream oil industry by introducing specific materials as sensing probes to discover the ambiguous aspect of reservoirs where no other technique is applicable [1,2]. Tracers have been used to respond to the uncertainties around the inter-well connections, vertical and horizontal flow path, and residual oil saturation by providing comprehensive data about reservoir conditions to control the oil displacement processes [3,4]. By development of waterflooding to extract remaining oil through enhanced oil recovery (EOR), chemical tracers have been much more interested than before in determining the EOR potential, time, and cost-effectiveness and evaluating the EOR-related factors before the oil field test [5–8].

As some parameters e.g., temperature, pressure, rock types and properties, ionic force, and biological activity have major influences on the oil reservoirs conditions, the success of a tracer test depends on finding appropriate materials to be stable and reliable in such harsh environmental conditions [9,10]. So, chemicals with the potential to meet these demands were being tested [11,12]. Radioactive isotopes are listed amongst the most used water tracer due to precise detection at the low dosage which required small injection quantities, negligible natural background in the environment; and also few reactions with the rock reservoirs [13]. However, by considering radiation hazards, toxicity, and short half-lives, non-radioactive chemical species including inorganic ions [11], alcohols [14,15], and fluorescent dyes [16] have been preferred to radioactive ones in the latter. Despite addresses radioactive shortcomings, long analysis time of ions, low-temperature stability and susceptibility to bacterial degradation of alcohols, and decomposition or adsorption of dyes on the rock reservoirs are discussed and not well documented which has opened up the way for further research [17]. With regards, there is a need to explore the materials with high colloidal, thermal, and physicochemical stability benefiting inertness; non-volatility, non-reactivity, and non-toxicity during injection without decomposing or adsorbing on the rock reservoirs to develop a new generation of tracer [18–20].

Since 2000, Nanoscience proposes encouraging mutations in a large number of domains, from fundamental studies to industrial research by presenting materials with nanometric dimensions and unique features [21–24]. Nanoparticles, with their outstanding electrical, optical, magnetic, and mechanical properties have gained relevance as the nano tracer for reservoir description via various instrumental methods [25,26]. Particularly, the nanoparticles are more focused which can be sustainable in the harsh reservoir conditions and on-site detectable with fast and reliable instruments to save analysis time without needing high equipped laboratory [27–30].

Carbon dots (CDs) as the zero-dimensional luminescent nanoparticles would be attractive candidates for this purpose by considering significant response in low concentrations that can be followed by ultrasensitive fluorescence spectroscopy, and fast response and portable UV cabinets [31,32]. Generally, CDs consist of a conjugated graphitic core covered by an amorphous carbon framework that is robust in harsh conditions and also a surface with hydrophilic functionalities which are responsible to enter CDs into the aqueous phase [33,34]. The size distribution and chemical composition of CDs can be tuned by regulating the thermal-pyrolysis conditions and type of used precursors which determine the final optical characteristics [35–37]. Specifically, the emission wavelength of CDs can shift from 430 to 600 nm by controlling the extent of core conjugation length and the density of surface functional groups to achieve multi-color emission that is favorable in sensing applications [38,39]. Moreover, benefitting nanometer size with a large surface area accelerates the migration of CDs through the tiny pores in reservoirs without notable interaction with the rock [40,41]. Given such behavior makes CDs competent as an enhancing agent to sweep oil from inaccessible sites of reservoirs by modifying the rheological parameters that influenced the oil recovery. Significant interactions with reservoir rocks and remaining oil to alter wettability, and reduce interfacial tension and mobility ratio are the major mechanisms that have been expected to extract oil with nanofluid-carrying CDs [19].

By merging the sensing and enhancing functions, CDs can be employed as the outstanding materials for nanofluid preparation for EOR applications. However, several experiments are necessary to be applied for clearly monitoring the flow path and understanding oil displacement by CDs tagged water. Regards, this study is devoted using of CDs with different optical properties and dual emissions (green and yellow denoted as g-CDs and y-CDs) for EOR studies by controlling graphitization and surface function through thermal pyrolysis of phenylenediamine derivatives focused on sustainability in the harsh temperature and salinity. Moreover, we demonstrated how tagging of water with CDs can help to understand and optimize EOR processes by following fluorescent emission, and controlling mobility ratio, wettability alteration, and interfacial tension by simulating oil-rock-fluid interactions on a micromodel platform.

Orto-phenylenediamine (o-PD), meta-phenylenediamine (m-PD), tartaric acid (TA), ethanol, sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), and magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. A 50.000 ppm brine solution including 25.000 ppm NaCl, 10.000 ppm MgCl2·6H2O, 10.000 ppm Na2SO4, and 5.000 ppm CaCl2 with a density of 1.03 g. mL−1 was used as a high salinity fluid to carry CDs. EOR studies were followed using crude oil of 21.01 °API with a density and molecular mass of 0.9279 g. mL−1 and 308 g. mol−1, and carbonate core samples from Ahwaz- Iran.

The physicochemical characteristics of the synthesized CDs were evaluated through AFM (Veeco CP Research instrument equipped with a silicon cantilever), TEM (PHILIPS CM 200), Raman (Almega Thermo Nicolet Dispersive Raman Spectrometer with a 532 nm of a Nd:YLF laser excitation), FT-IR (Bruker Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrophotometer), DLS and Zeta potential (Zetasizer Nano ZS device equipped with He-Ne laser radiation λ = 632.8 nm), and TGA (Mettler Toledo 851 under N2 atmosphere). Moreover, the optical characteristics were monitored by a UV − vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 60) and fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer-LS55). Naked-eye detection of the CDs properties was done inside the cabinet under UV radiation with a wavelength of 365 nm and images were recorded by the Samsung Galaxy J5 Pro mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EOR nanofluid and core samples preparation
ce:section.ce:para: CDs with dual colors emission were synthesized using PD isomers in the absence and presence of TA via the solvothermal method in ethanol. Typically, 0.45 g o-PD was dissolved in 45 mL ethanol for the synthesis of yellow emissive CDs (y-CDs) by transferring the mixture into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and heated to 180 °C for 10 h. The reaction solution was cooled down to room temperature and purified using a dialysis bag (cut-off 3000 Da) against DW for a week to collect dark orange suspension. Moreover, green emissive CDs (g-CDs) were obtained in the same procedure by dissolving 0.90 g m-PD and 1.2 g TA in 90 mL ethanol and collecting dark green colloidal suspension [42]. EOR nanofluids incorporate y-CDs with photoluminescence quantum yields (QY) of 18 % and g-CDs with QY of 30 % (compared to Rhodamine B as a standard material) with different concentrations were prepared by diluting the original suspension of CDs in 50.000 ppm brine water followed by naked-eye detection and zeta potential determination to control the colloidal stability before EOR studies to prevent clogging the rock pores.

Carbonate reservoir rock was cut as thin cylinders, washed with toluene/methanol and DW water, and aged in crude oil for one month to be oil-wet for EOR studies. Oil recovery was followed by exposing the prepared cores in nanofluids containing g-CDs and y-CDs for 24 h by controlling the wettability alteration from oil-wet to water-wet via measurement contact angle. Moreover, the degree of nanofluids inhibition by rock reservoirs was studied by monitoring the fluorescence emission of CDs after injection to the oil-wet core samples.

Investigation of CD's impact on the water/oil immiscible two-phase system was controlled via the addition of each CDs by desired concentrations to the mixture. Regard, the different volume ratios of nanofluid/ crude oil were mixed by shaking vigorously for five minutes and keeping at reservoir temperature for 2 h. The water/oil two-phase equilibrium in the presence of each CDs, as well as emission stability, was followed visually and by recording fluorescence spectra.

Wettability alteration was considered by Contact angle measurement through the static sessile drop method using DSA apparatus equipped with a light source, a transparent chamber consisting of DW and the core compartment, an oil injection system, and a camera to record the images. The wettability of core samples in each step of EOR studies was controlled by releasing an oil drop on the core at ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure, imaged by a camera, and analyzed by ImageJ software. IFT was measured using pendant drop method by placing oil drop in nanofluids matrices.

CDs capability to oil displacement on the porous media of glass micromodel as a two-dimensional simulator of oil reservoir rock was investigated by taking images during the nanofluids injection and movement. Oil displacement factor for two nano-fluids incorporating g-CDs and y-CDs was estimated with ImageJ software which has the ability to discriminate colors of nanofluids from oil. Micromodel tests were run by cleaning the flow paths with toluene, methanol, and water followed by drying in the oven. The oil-wet micromodel was prepared by injecting crude oil until saturation and keeping it at 75 °C as reservoir temperature for two weeks. Afterward, the Oil displacement was followed by pumping nanofluids to flow on the oil-wet micromodel paths at an injection rate of 0.025 mL/hour. Images were taken during nanofluids injection to monitor the fluid movement, calculate the oil displacement factor, and investigate the EOR mechanism.

The spherical morphology with uniform distribution was observed in AFM images (Fig. 1 a and b) for both CDs with a mean particle size around 5 and 8 nm for g-CDs, and y-CDs respectively, and TEM image in Fig. 1c confirmed the spherical particles for g-CDs. As a supplementary analysis (Fig. S1, Supplementary Information), the DLS data revealed the hydrodynamic diameter of ca. 6 and 12 nm for g-CDs and y-CDs which is in good consistent with values determined by AFM, and TEM. The presence of a low intense peak at 60 nm for y-CDs may be attributed to the larger particles formation or their aggregation in DW. Particle colloidal stability and surface charge were also undertaken using zeta potential analyzes to consider any aggregation or sedimentation of EOR fluid before operations. The average surface charge of +16.6 and −8.02 recorded for g-CDs and y-CDs confirmed the appropriate colloidal stability in solution albeit, g-CDs present better stability compared to y-CDs which is more desirable to prepare EOR nanofluid (Fig. S2). The positive surface charge of g-CDs is attributed to the protonation of the amine functional groups and the formation of NH3 + at pH = 7.5 [43,44].

The Raman spectra for both CDs in Fig. 1d exhibit two peaks at 1346 and 1517 cm−1 corresponding to the D band (disordered, sp3 hybridization) and G band (graphite, sp2 hybridization) of carbon materials. As it is known, the ratio of ID/IG is characteristic of the defect degree and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon which calculated 0.96 and 0.68 for g-CDs and y-CDs respectively. The decrease ratio value implies the increasing graphite content in the CDs from green to yellow, indicating that the graphitization of CDs is enhanced in yellow-emissive CDs [45]. While the existence of a large fraction of defects in g-CDs was recorded as a result of surface oxidation in the presence of tartaric acid. Chemical structure and also surface functional groups investigation were followed by FT-IR analysis for synthesized CDs with green and yellow emissions (Fig. 1e). Recorded FT-IR spectrum for y-CDs confirmed the presence of CH bending (783 cm−1), symmetric stretching CNHC (1150 cm−1), NH2 stretching aromatic ring (1320–1240 cm−1), stretching CN (1460 cm−1), symmetric stretching CH (2790, 2890 cm−1), stretching CN and CC (1600 cm−1), stretching CO (1670 cm−1), NH2 bending Overton (3200 cm−1), symmetric NH2 (3330 cm−1) and stretching NH (3430 cm−1) vibrations. While for g-CDs, characteristic peaks at 1730 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1 in the recorded spectrum belong to the stretching vibrations CO and OH, which support the formation of the carboxylic group in the presence of tartaric acid. The obtained FT-IR spectra proved that difference in the chemical structure of the synthesized CDs is responsible for the difference in their emissions [46]. More details about chemical bonds and functional groups of CDs were reported by Jiang et al. [42] by XPS analysis which show that the oxidation degree and carboxylation were obviously enhanced when tartaric acid was added during the preparation of CDs by the formation of CO and CO bonds for g-CDs compare to y-CDs.

To further characterization, the thermal stability of CDs was studied by monitoring the weight losses of the samples during temperature increments using the TGA technique (Fig. 1f). For both types of CDs, a constant plateau in the temperature range of 25–150 °C confirmed the appropriate stability of CDs in the actual reservoir temperature. The one weight loss step in the temperature range of 150–250 °C for g-CDs and two steps at 150–200 °C and 300–350 °C for y-CDs is attributed to the removal of water molecules and functional groups on the surface of the CDs. TGA profiles recorded for the g-CDs and y-CDs showed different thermal behavior, which emphasizes their different structures.

Evaluation of the tracer behavior before injecting into the reservoirs is a required step in any tracer test. Regards, the optical properties of the synthesized CDs were controlled by naked-eye and fluorescence spectroscopy. The results depicted in Fig. 2 a for CDs synthesized by m-PD and TA precursors show the two absorption bands centered at 295 and 455 nm, assigned to π-π* transitions of the graphitic core and n-π* transitions of the surface functional groups respectively. While the fluorescence spectrum shows the highest emission at 500 nm under a 365 nm irradiation which confirms the green luminescence emitted by CDs in DW. The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra recorded for the CDs obtained by o-PD also show the maximum absorption at 290 and 427 nm and the highest emission at 545 nm which gives confirmation of yellow emission by CDs (Fig. 2b) [45].

To determine the appropriate CDs concentration as a sensing agent, different concentrations were prepared by diluting synthesized stocks (20.000 ppm for g-CDs and 4.000for y-CDs) in DW and followed by naked-eye under a UV lamp irradiated at 365 nm. The taken images (Fig. 2c) show the highest emission intensities were obtained for 200 ppm and then 20 ppm of g-CDs. The emission of higher concentrations was restricted by self-absorption while a reduction in the number of nanoparticles in the solution is responsible for significantly quenching emission at lower concentrations (2 ppm). For y-CDs, although higher concentrations may provide more intense emissions, due to the cost-effectiveness and minimum injection of materials into the ground, subsequent studies were conducted on 200 and 20 ppm (Fig. 2d).

Before EOR operation, successful transport and breakthrough of CDs through reservoirs were monitored at high salinities and temperatures to prevent any side effects and aggregation. The salt tolerance capability of luminescent CDs with green and yellow emissions in the prepared nanofluids (synthetic brine with 50.000 ppm salinity) was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs with 20 and 200 ppm concentrations in DW and brine under the same conditions. For both types of CDs, the recorded spectra (Fig. 3 a, b) and taken images under UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm show the stability and high tolerance of CDs emissions in the presence of different ions with high concentrations. Fluorescence intensities of 95 % and 92 % were obtained for g-CDs and y-CDs to confirm the great emission stability during the injection process.

Next, the thermal stability of the CDs was also studied by the measurement of the emission intensity of the nanofluids carrying g-CDs and y-CDs with concentrations of 20 ppm and 200 ppm at 20 °C and 75 °C. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3c, d) and their corresponding images recorded under UV lamp irradiation for each CDs (Fig. S3 a, b) confirm the high-temperature stability without considerable emission quenching and any sedimentation at the oil reservoirs condition. The fluorescence response of g-CDs and y-CDs during one month was also controlled to investigate the long-time stability of CDs without quenching or aggregation as the most important parameter in water tracing. Fluorescence intensity spectra over time were recorded for EOR nanofluids (prepared by 20 and 200 ppm of g-CDs and y-CDs) to show stability for one month just by negligible changes in the fluorescence response (Fig. 4 a, b). The images recorded under UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm confirmed the spectra very well (Fig. 4 c, d). Accordingly, both synthesized CDs can be used for a long-time during the tracing process.

Before tracking CDs in crude oil samples at laboratory and field scale, the compatibility of the synthesized g-CDs and y-CDs with the water/oil immiscible two-phase system was studied in the separate experiments. For this, brine consisting of CDs with 20 and 200 ppm concentrations were added to crude oil with different volume ratios (25:75, 50:50, 75:25), shaken for 5 min, and kept for 2 h in reservoirs temperature [47]. The equilibrium of the immiscible water/oil two phases system was followed by naked-eye and confirmed by recording the fluorescence spectra to evaluate the performance of CDs in the real sample matrices. Images collected under visible light and UV lamp irradiate at 365 nm for both CDs with 20 (Fig. S4) and 200 ppm (Fig. 5 a, b) concentrations show that in the presence of CDs the equilibrium of immiscible water/oil system is deployed without formation of the third phase, emulsion, and aggregation of particles in the mixture. The stability of CDs fluorescence emission after exposure to the complex oil matrices was examined by recording fluorescence spectra of the aqueous phase sampled from the mixture. Analysis of the g-CDs and y-CDs (Fig. 5c, d) responses show slight changes (less than 15 % for different concentrations) in the CDs emission after injecting into the brine, keeping at a high temperature, and interacting with crude oil compared to injecting in DW at ambient temperature. The results of these experiments also showed that nanofluids containing green and yellow CDs with 200 ppm concentration are more easily detectable by naked-eye compared to 20 ppm and their emission intensity is less affected by temperature and high salinity water. Regards, 200 ppm concentration of g-CDs and y-CDs were studied as sensing agents for EOR operations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tractability of CDs in lab scale
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the synthesized CDs as sensing agents was controlled on the laboratory scale by injecting nanofluids incorporating 200 ppm of each CDs into carbonate reservoir rocks. Taken photos under UV light irradiation at 365 nm in Fig. 6 a show that the nanofluids carrying CDs on the oil-wet core samples are easily detectable by the naked eye without using the high-equipped instrument. Moreover, the recorded fluorescence spectra for nanofluids (Fig. 6b) after 24 h of interaction with the oil-wet cores show the small inhibition of CDs by the reservoir rock. With regards to the sensing experiments results, g-CDs show higher fluorescence emission intensity than y-CDs, which makes it desirable for fast monitoring of injected water just with a portable UV cabinet without employing complex equipment. Further, the degree of inhibition for g-CDs with core samples is less than y-CDs as a result of less fluorescence intensity quenching which confirmed the better performance of g-CDs for the tracing operations.

The viscosity of the EOR nanofluids e.g., brine, and brine contained 20 and 200 ppm concentrations of g-CDs and y-CDs was analyzed to study the CDs effects on the mobility ratio of water/oil and oil sweeping from reservoirs. The results recorded in Fig. S4 show that the addition of y-CDs had no strong influence on the brine viscosity, while the g-CDs performed a better operation and are expected to be more capable to recovered oil by controlling the mobility ratio mechanism as a result of increasing the nanofluid viscosity. According to the results, nanofluid carrying 200 ppm of g-CDs shows a better performance than the others to sweep oil by increasing the interaction time with crude oil remaining in the reservoirs to recover it.

A comprehensive study of rheological parameters was continued by controlling the wettability of reservoir rocks in the presence of CDs. The images recorded in Fig. 7 a-f show the changes in the contact angle as a criterion of the wettability for the oil-wet reservoir rocks in different steps after interaction with the EOR fluids. As shown in the figures, the presence of the CDs in the brine reduced the contact angle of injected oil with the treated rocks as a result of altering wettability from oil-wet to water-wet [48]. For g-CDs, the concentration of 200 ppm altered the wettability more significantly than 20 ppm, while the performance of both concentrations for y-CDs is similar. Adsorption and electrostatic interaction of brine ions, and CDs with the rock and oil carboxylic acid groups are responsible for wettability alteration. Regard to the positive surface charge of g-CDs proved with the zeta potential, the proposed mechanism to alter the wettability is based on a strong electrostatic force between positive charges of nanoparticles with carboxylic acid groups of crude oil to separate it from reservoir rocks. While the electrostatic interactions between the negative charge of y-CDs and the positive charge of carbonate rocks are responsible of sweep oil by y-CDs adsorption on the rock to release the remaining oil. The comparison of these two types of CDs with different surface charges revealed that the electrostatic interactions between positive surface charge CDs and negative carboxylic acid groups of crude oil are stronger than the electrostatic force between negative surface charge CDs and carbonate rocks.

The distribution and displacement of fluids in porous media were studied by measurement of the IFT for the prepared nanofluids and crude oil. For this purpose, an oil drop was released into the medium of the brine sample, and the brine containing 20 ppm concentrations of g-CDs and y-CDs in separate experiments to analyze the drop shape and calculate the IFT. The trend of the IFT data in Fig. 7g-i confirmed that the high concentration of ions used to prepare brine show acceptable influences to reduce the IFT, however further decreases obtained by the addition of CDs to brine as a result of large surface area which leads to improves the nanofluid/crude oil contact surface to more oil movement and recovery.

Glass micromodel was used to simulate nanofluids and oil movement in the reservoirs, displacement mechanism evaluation, and also displacement factor calculation. Experiments performed in the micromodel also provide opportunities to compare the performance of different CDs and their effect on oil recovery. For this, three fluids e.g., brine, and brine containing 200 ppm concentrations of each CDs were prepared and injected into the porous medium of the oil-wet micro model to study the fluid's behavior. Images from the beginning and end of the brine injection stage depicted in Fig. 8 a, and b confirmed that the oil-wet paths have been partially recovered by brine with an 11 % displacement factor. In separate operations, after injection of g-CDs and y-CDs into the micromodel in the same conditions, the obtained images were analyzed with ImageJ software, and the displacement factor 20.5 % and 27.8 %was calculated for g-CDs and y-CDs respectively (Fig. 8c and d). Also, the magnified images of the micromodel in the absence and presence of CDs (Fig. S5) show that in the brine injection step the oil-wet walls of the micromodel have not significantly changed and the brine only moved through the middle of the flow paths. By passing nanofluids on the micromodel, the wettability of the walls significantly altered to water-wet which cause to sweep of oil from the wall and further improvement of the oil recovery yield.

In this study, a controllable and efficient solvothermal method was used to synthesize dual-color emissive CDs (green, and yellow) by taking m-PD and o-PD in the presence and absence of TA. Using the different PD derivatives, and the addition of TA changed the degree of surface oxidation and carboxyl groups formation which led to emit different colors by the prepared CDs. Derived fluorescent-tunable CDs from low-cost and commercially available PD precursors can be utilized as the new luminescent type of probes to characterize oil reservoirs conditions before EOR operations to save cost and time of projects. Sustainable absorbance and fluorescent properties in high salinity and temperature with easily-detected approaches, long-time stability, swift migration potential in porous media, and capability to manipulate oil-rock-fluid interactions make these prepared CDs more convenient to both sensing and enhancing functions in EOR operations. According to our results, nanofluid carrying g-CDs swept oil by decreasing mobility ratio, altering rock wettability to water-wet, and reducing IFT while y-CDs took advantage of the last two parameters. Finally, sweeping crude oil by each prepared nanofluid was monitored on the micromodel pattern to propose the displacement mechanism obtained by CDs. The oil displacement factor was calculated 20.5 % and 27.8 % for g-CDs and y-CDs respectively.

Saba Ranjbar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Nahid Sarlak: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Alimorad Rashidi: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to acknowledge the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF, Grant No. 99002385) for financial support of this project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.07.109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Passive organic cationic tracers for carbonate formations: Development and performance at oil reservoir emulating conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Maldonado-Domínguez, O. González-Antonio, L.E. Díaz-Paulino, R.C. Cañas-Alonso, E. Xochitiotzi-Flores, C.A. Jiménez-García, M.A. Almaraz-Girón, V.M. Luna-Pabello, M. Romero-Ávila, F.
